name: Kyrgyzstan
tagline: From the Yenisei steppe and the epic of Manas to a turbulent mountain democracy.
summary: Kyrgyzstan's story begins with the Yenisei Kyrgyz of the Siberian steppe, whose khaganate destroyed the Uyghur capital in 840, and with the great oral epic of Manas that carried the people's memory across centuries. Along the Silk Road through the Tian Shan rose Karakhanid Balasagun, the sacred mountain of Sulaiman-Too above Osh, and the caravan halts of Lake Issyk-Kul, while Mongol conquest and later pressures drew the Kyrgyz tribes into the Tian Shan that became their homeland. Absorbed by the Khanate of Kokand and then by the Russian Empire, the Kyrgyz rose in the catastrophic 1916 revolt before being organized as the Kara-Kyrgyz Autonomous Oblast and the Kirghiz SSR of the Soviet Union. Kyrgyzstan declared independence on 31 August 1991, and since then has lived a turbulent, competitive politics — the 2005 Tulip Revolution, the 2010 uprising and the ethnic violence in the south, and a return to a presidential republic in 2021.

founding.label: Independence from the Soviet Union
founding.detail: Modern Kyrgyzstan dates its statehood to 31 August 1991, when the Supreme Soviet of the republic declared independence as the Soviet Union disintegrated in the wake of the failed August coup in Moscow. Askar Akayev, a physicist who had been chosen head of the republic in 1990, became the first president of independent Kyrgyzstan, and the Soviet Union was formally dissolved at the end of December 1991. The deeper roots of Kyrgyz identity reach back more than a thousand years to the Yenisei Kyrgyz of the steppe and to the epic of Manas, which tells of the unification of scattered tribes into one people. Independence in 1991 gave a sovereign state to a nation that had spent the previous century under the rule of the Khanate of Kokand, the Russian Empire and the Soviet Union, and whose homeland in the Tian Shan it now governs from Bishkek.

quickFacts.label: Steppe origins
quickFacts.value: The Yenisei Kyrgyz, whose khaganate destroyed the Uyghur capital Ordu-Baliq in 840

quickFacts.label: National epic
quickFacts.value: Manas, a vast oral trilogy inscribed by UNESCO in 2013; the longest version runs to some half a million lines

quickFacts.label: Silk Road heritage
quickFacts.value: Karakhanid Balasagun and the Burana tower, Lake Issyk-Kul, and the sacred mountain of Sulaiman-Too at Osh (UNESCO, 2009)

quickFacts.label: Russian rule
quickFacts.value: Conquered with the Khanate of Kokand in 1876; the 1916 revolt (Urkun) drove tens of thousands into China

quickFacts.label: Soviet republic
quickFacts.value: Kara-Kyrgyz Autonomous Oblast (1924); a full union republic, the Kirghiz SSR, from 1936

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Declared 31 August 1991; capital Bishkek (the former Frunze)

eras.title: The Yenisei Kyrgyz and the Epic of Manas
eras.standfirst: An ancient Turkic people of the upper Yenisei built a steppe power that briefly dominated Mongolia, and carried into later centuries the vast oral epic of Manas.
body: The Kyrgyz are among the oldest named peoples of Inner Asia. The Yenisei Kyrgyz dwelled along the upper reaches of the Yenisei River, in the Minusinsk Basin of southern Siberia, and appear in early Chinese and Turkic records as a Turkic-speaking people of herders, hunters and metalworkers. For centuries they lived under or alongside the great steppe powers — the Turkic and then the Uyghur khaganates — before rising to dominance of their own in the ninth century. Their long presence on the Yenisei, far from the Tian Shan that would later become the Kyrgyz homeland, makes the migration southward one of the defining threads of the nation's history.

In 840 the Yenisei Kyrgyz overthrew the Uyghur Khaganate, sacking its capital Ordu-Baliq in the Orkhon valley and driving the Uyghurs out of Mongolia. For a time the Kyrgyz khaganate was the leading power of the eastern steppe, its authority reaching from the Yenisei toward Central Asia, though it did not hold this dominance for long. The episode is remembered as the high point of the early Kyrgyz state and as the moment the people entered the front rank of steppe history, before the rise of the Mongols a few centuries later transformed the political map of Inner Asia once again.

Out of this deep past the Kyrgyz carried their greatest cultural treasure: the epic of Manas. A vast oral poem recited by trained bards, the manaschi, it tells of the hero Manas and his unification of the scattered Kyrgyz tribes into a single people, and continues in the lives of his son Semetey and grandson Seytek. The longest recorded versions run to hundreds of thousands of lines, and a full recital can last many hours. Transmitted by memory across generations, the epic preserves the historical imagination, customs and worldview of the Kyrgyz, and was inscribed on UNESCO's list of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2013.

events.title: The Yenisei Kyrgyz destroy the Uyghur Khaganate
events.summary: The Yenisei Kyrgyz overthrew the Uyghur Khaganate and sacked its capital Ordu-Baliq, briefly making the Kyrgyz the dominant power of the eastern steppe and driving the Uyghurs out of Mongolia.

events.title: Transmission of the epic of Manas
events.summary: The Kyrgyz preserved the vast oral epic of Manas, recited by bards (manaschi), telling of the legendary hero who united the Kyrgyz tribes; it remains the cornerstone of Kyrgyz cultural memory.

events.title: Manas trilogy recognized by UNESCO
events.summary: UNESCO inscribed the Kyrgyz epic trilogy of Manas, Semetey and Seytek on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, recognizing one of the world's longest oral epics.

figures.name: Manas
figures.blurb: The central hero of the Kyrgyz national epic, Manas is celebrated as the warrior who united the scattered Kyrgyz tribes into a single people; the forty rays of the sun on the national flag are said to represent the forty tribes he brought together.

pullquote.text: The Kyrgyz epic trilogy of Manas, Semetey and Seytek expresses the historical memory of the Kyrgyz people and survives thanks to a community of epic tellers.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO, on the Kyrgyz epic trilogy

eras.title: The Silk Road, the Tian Shan and the Mongol Age
eras.standfirst: Caravan cities and sacred mountains flourished along the Silk Road through the Tian Shan, while Mongol conquest and later upheavals drew the Kyrgyz into the mountains that became their home.
body: The valleys of the Tian Shan and the shores of Lake Issyk-Kul lay athwart the Silk Road, the network of routes that carried goods, faiths and ideas between China and the West. In the Chüy valley the Karakhanid dynasty, the first Turkic Muslim power of Central Asia, made Balasagun one of its capitals; its surviving minaret, the Burana tower, still rises from the ruined city near modern Tokmok. Lake Issyk-Kul, a vast warm-water lake high in the mountains that never freezes, was a caravan halt with lakeside settlements, while above the town of Osh in the Fergana valley the five-peaked massif of Sulaiman-Too was venerated by travellers and pilgrims for more than a millennium.

These sites bear witness to a long history of exchange and devotion. Sulaiman-Too blends pre-Islamic and Islamic worship, its slopes carved with petroglyphs and crowned by reconstructed sixteenth-century mosques; in 2009 it became Kyrgyzstan's first UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Silk Road corridor through the Tian Shan, including the Chüy-valley cities, was recognized in 2014 within the transnational World Heritage property 'Silk Roads: the Routes Network of Chang'an-Tianshan Corridor', shared by China, Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan. Together they mark the Kyrgyz lands as a meeting place of nomadic and settled, Turkic and Persian, Buddhist, Nestorian Christian and Muslim worlds.

The thirteenth century brought the Mongol storm. Genghis Khan's empire swept across Central Asia, and the Kyrgyz lands were drawn into the Mongol orbit and, after the empire's division, into the Chagatai realm and its successors. Over the following centuries, amid the rise and fall of steppe powers and the pressure of rival nomads such as the Oirat (Dzungar) Mongols, the Kyrgyz tribes consolidated in the Tian Shan, the high mountain country that became their permanent homeland. By the seventeenth century the Kyrgyz were established as mountain nomads of the Tian Shan, organized in tribes and clans rather than a single state.

events.title: Karakhanid Balasagun and the Burana tower
events.summary: Under the Karakhanid dynasty, the first Turkic Muslim power in Central Asia, Balasagun in the Chüy valley flourished as a capital; its minaret, the Burana tower, survives as the best-known monument of northern Kyrgyzstan.

events.title: Mongol conquest of Central Asia
events.summary: The empire of Genghis Khan overran Central Asia, drawing the Kyrgyz lands into the Mongol world and, after the empire's division, into the Chagatai realm and its successor states.

events.title: Kyrgyz consolidate in the Tian Shan
events.summary: Through the upheavals of the post-Mongol centuries the Kyrgyz tribes settled in the Tian Shan, living as mountain nomads organized in tribes and clans, the homeland they hold to this day.

events.title: Sulaiman-Too inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: The sacred mountain of Sulaiman-Too above Osh, with its petroglyphs, places of worship and reconstructed 16th-century mosques, became Kyrgyzstan's first UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognized for its blend of pre-Islamic and Islamic belief.

pullquote.text: Sulaiman-Too Sacred Mountain dominates the Fergana Valley and forms the backdrop to the city of Osh, at the crossroads of important routes on the Central Asian Silk Roads.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, on Sulaiman-Too

eras.title: The Khanate of Kokand and the Russian Conquest
eras.standfirst: The Kyrgyz fell under the Khanate of Kokand and then under the Russian Empire, whose colonial settlement provoked the catastrophic revolt of 1916.
body: In the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries the Kyrgyz tribes of the Tian Shan and the Fergana region were drawn into the orbit of the Khanate of Kokand, the Uzbek state centred on the Fergana valley. Kokand built the fortress of Pishpek — the future Bishkek — to control the Chüy valley, and recruited Kyrgyz horsemen into its armies, but its rule was resented and frequently resisted. The Kyrgyz, divided among many tribes and never united in a single state, navigated between Kokand, the Chinese Qing empire to the east and, increasingly, the advancing Russian Empire to the north.

From the 1850s Russian forces pushed into Central Asia, and many northern Kyrgyz accepted Russian protection as a counterweight to Kokand. The conquest reached its climax in 1876, when Russia abolished the Khanate of Kokand after suppressing a major uprising and annexed its territory, organizing the lands as the Fergana Province within the Turkestan Governorate-General. The Kyrgyz country thus passed under direct imperial rule. Russian and Slavic settlers were encouraged to colonize the fertile valleys and pastures, and large tracts of land used by the nomads for grazing were taken for peasant colonists, building deep resentment among the Kyrgyz.

That resentment erupted in 1916. When Tsar Nicholas II decreed the conscription of Central Asians for labour service behind the front lines of the First World War, it set off a vast revolt across Russian Turkestan, remembered by the Kyrgyz as the Urkun, the 'exodus'. In the Semirechye and the Tian Shan, Russian troops and armed settlers crushed the rising with great violence; tens of thousands of Kyrgyz who survived fled across the high passes toward China, and many died of cold, hunger and exhaustion in the mountains. Estimates of the dead and the displaced are uncertain, but by some accounts a large share of the northern Kyrgyz population perished — a trauma that scarred the nation's memory.

events.title: Kyrgyz under the Khanate of Kokand
events.summary: The Khanate of Kokand extended its rule over the Kyrgyz tribes, building the fortress of Pishpek (later Bishkek) to control the Chüy valley and recruiting Kyrgyz fighters, though its rule was widely resisted.

events.title: Russia annexes Kokand; Kyrgyzstan under imperial rule
events.summary: After suppressing an uprising, the Russian Empire abolished the Khanate of Kokand and annexed its lands as the Fergana Province, bringing the Kyrgyz country under direct Russian colonial rule.

events.title: The 1916 revolt (Urkun)
events.summary: A decree conscripting Central Asians for wartime labour triggered a revolt across Russian Turkestan; in the Kyrgyz lands it was suppressed with great violence, and tens of thousands fled toward China, with heavy loss of life. Casualty figures remain estimates.

pullquote.text: In the eastern part of what was then Russian Turkestan, tens of thousands of Kyrgyz and Kazakhs who were not killed in the fighting fled toward China.

eras.title: Soviet Kirghizia
eras.standfirst: After the Russian Revolution the Kyrgyz were given their own Soviet territory, from an autonomous oblast to a full union republic, transformed by collectivization, industry and a literate national culture.
body: The Russian Revolution of 1917 and the civil war that followed brought Soviet power to Central Asia. As the Bolsheviks consolidated control, they reorganized the region along national lines. In the great national-territorial delimitation of 1924, the predominantly Kyrgyz lands were constituted as the Kara-Kyrgyz Autonomous Oblast within the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic — the first time the Kyrgyz had a territory bearing their own name. It was renamed the Kyrgyz Autonomous Oblast, raised to an autonomous republic in 1926, and in 1936 became the Kirghiz Soviet Socialist Republic, a full constituent republic of the Soviet Union, with its capital at Frunze (the renamed Pishpek).

Soviet rule transformed Kyrgyz society profoundly and often brutally. The forced collectivization of agriculture from the late 1920s shattered the nomadic herding economy and brought hardship and famine, while the purges of the 1930s destroyed much of the new Kyrgyz political and cultural elite. Yet the same decades brought mass literacy, schools and universities, written Kyrgyz literature, public health and the industrial development of mining and manufacturing. A modern Kyrgyz nation took shape within the Soviet framework, its borders, institutions and capital established by the Soviet state.

The republic produced figures of international stature, above all the writer Chingiz Aitmatov, whose novels and stories — among them Jamila and The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years — won fame across the Soviet Union and the world and gave the Kyrgyz a voice in modern literature. As the reforms of Mikhail Gorbachev loosened the Soviet system in the late 1980s, Kyrgyzstan, like the other republics, moved toward self-assertion. In 1990 the physicist Askar Akayev was chosen to head the republic, and as the Soviet Union collapsed the stage was set for independence.

events.title: Kara-Kyrgyz Autonomous Oblast created
events.summary: In the Soviet national-territorial delimitation of Central Asia, the Kyrgyz lands were organized as the Kara-Kyrgyz Autonomous Oblast within the Russian SFSR, giving the Kyrgyz their own named territory for the first time.

events.title: The Kirghiz SSR becomes a union republic
events.summary: Kyrgyzstan was elevated to the Kirghiz Soviet Socialist Republic, a full constituent republic of the Soviet Union, with its capital at Frunze (formerly Pishpek, today Bishkek).

events.title: Chingiz Aitmatov wins the Lenin Prize
events.summary: The Kyrgyz writer Chingiz Aitmatov was awarded the Lenin Prize for 'Tales of the Mountains and Steppes', bringing Kyrgyz literature to a worldwide audience and becoming the republic's most celebrated cultural figure.

events.title: Akayev chosen to lead the republic
events.summary: Amid Gorbachev's reforms and rising ethnic tension in the south, the physicist Askar Akayev was elected head of the Kirghiz SSR, the leader who would guide the republic to independence.

figures.name: Chingiz Aitmatov
figures.blurb: Kyrgyzstan's most celebrated author, who wrote in both Kyrgyz and Russian. Works such as Jamila and The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years won him the Lenin Prize (1963) and worldwide renown; he later served as a diplomat for the Soviet Union and independent Kyrgyzstan.

pullquote.text: Although Aytmatov composed in both Russian and Kyrgyz, many of his works were originally written in the latter; his themes were love and friendship, the heroism of wartime, and the emancipation of Kyrgyz youth from restrictive custom.

eras.title: Independence and the Akayev Years
eras.standfirst: Kyrgyzstan declared independence on 31 August 1991 and, under President Askar Akayev, won a reputation as Central Asia's most open society before sliding toward corruption and revolt.
body: On 31 August 1991, as the Soviet Union fell apart after the failed Moscow coup, the republic's Supreme Soviet declared the independence of Kyrgyzstan. Askar Akayev, who had become the republic's leader the year before, was confirmed as the first president of the new state, and at the end of December 1991 the Soviet Union was formally dissolved. The United States and other powers extended recognition: Washington established diplomatic relations with Kyrgyzstan on 25 December 1991 and opened its embassy in Bishkek the following February. The capital, renamed Bishkek from Frunze, became the seat of a small, mountainous and landlocked republic feeling its way as a sovereign nation.

In its first years independent Kyrgyzstan stood out among the Central Asian states. Under Akayev the country adopted a liberal constitution, allowed a relatively free press and an active civil society, embraced market reforms and welcomed foreign engagement, earning a reputation as an 'island of democracy' in an authoritarian region. It joined the United Nations and the international financial institutions and, after the attacks of 11 September 2001, hosted both an American and a Russian military base in support of operations in neighbouring Afghanistan.

Over time, however, the promise faded. Akayev's rule grew more authoritarian, power and wealth concentrated around his family, and elections were marred by accusations of fraud and the harassment of the opposition. Poverty, unemployment and a sharp divide between the more Russified north and the conservative, densely populated south deepened public discontent. The flawed parliamentary elections of early 2005 became the spark: mass protests, beginning in the south, swept the country and forced Akayev to flee, ending the career of independent Kyrgyzstan's first president.

events.title: Kyrgyzstan declares independence
events.summary: As the Soviet Union collapsed, the Supreme Soviet of the republic declared Kyrgyzstan's independence; Askar Akayev became the first president of the sovereign state, with its capital at Bishkek.

events.title: U.S. recognition and diplomatic relations
events.summary: The United States established diplomatic relations with independent Kyrgyzstan on 25 December 1991, opening its embassy in Bishkek in February 1992 as the Soviet Union was dissolved.

events.title: The Tulip Revolution
events.summary: Allegations of fraud in the parliamentary elections and of pervasive corruption sparked mass demonstrations that forced President Akayev to flee the country, in the first of Kyrgyzstan's post-Soviet revolutions.

figures.name: Askar Akayev
figures.blurb: A physicist who led the republic from 1990, Akayev became independent Kyrgyzstan's first president in 1991 and presided over its early reputation as Central Asia's most open society; accusations of corruption and electoral fraud led to his overthrow in the 2005 Tulip Revolution.

pullquote.text: Allegations of government corruption and of vote rigging in the parliamentary election sparked widespread demonstrations, and in March 2005 Akayev was forced to flee the country.

eras.title: A Turbulent Republic: Revolution, Violence and Competitive Politics
eras.standfirst: Two more upheavals, a tragic outbreak of ethnic violence in the south, and a swing back to presidential rule have marked Kyrgyzstan's turbulent but uniquely competitive politics.
body: The Tulip Revolution brought Kurmanbek Bakiyev to power, but the new government soon reproduced the failings of the old. Concentration of power and wealth around the president's family, rising prices and renewed accusations of corruption fed popular anger, and in April 2010 a violent uprising in Bishkek left dozens dead and drove Bakiyev from office. An interim government led by Roza Otunbayeva took charge and oversaw a new constitution that turned Kyrgyzstan into a parliamentary republic — an exceptional choice in a region of strong presidencies. When Otunbayeva stepped down after the presidential election of 2011, it marked the first peaceful transfer of power in the country's history.

The collapse of authority in 2010 had a tragic sequel in the south. In June, clashes between ethnic Kyrgyz and the large Uzbek minority in the cities of Osh and Jalal-Abad exploded into days of communal violence, with killings, arson and the destruction of whole neighbourhoods. Official figures put the dead at around four hundred, while other estimates ran considerably higher; all such figures are estimates, and the toll remains contested. Several hundred thousand people were displaced, many of them Uzbeks who fled toward Uzbekistan. The violence, treated soberly in its aftermath, exposed the depth of inter-ethnic tension in the densely populated Fergana valley and left lasting scars on the south.

Kyrgyzstan's politics remained more open and contested than those of its neighbours, but also more unstable. In 2020, disputed parliamentary elections triggered a third wave of mass protests; the nationalist politician Sadyr Japarov, freed from prison amid the unrest, rose swiftly to the presidency, winning election in January 2021. Constitutional referendums in 2021 restored a strong presidential system, reversing the parliamentary model of 2010 and reducing the powers of the legislature. Into the mid-2020s Kyrgyzstan has continued to balance its ties between Russia, China and the West, governing a young and turbulent democracy from Bishkek while honouring the steppe heritage and the epic of Manas at the heart of its national identity.

events.title: The 2010 revolution ousts Bakiyev
events.summary: A violent uprising in Bishkek over corruption and rising prices forced President Kurmanbek Bakiyev to flee; an interim government under Roza Otunbayeva took power and prepared a new constitution.

events.title: Ethnic violence in the south
events.summary: Clashes between ethnic Kyrgyz and Uzbeks in Osh and Jalal-Abad erupted into days of communal violence, killing several hundred people — figures are estimates and remain contested — and displacing hundreds of thousands.

events.title: Parliamentary republic and the first peaceful transfer
events.summary: A new constitution made Kyrgyzstan a parliamentary republic, a rarity in Central Asia; the 2011 election that followed Otunbayeva's interim presidency was the country's first peaceful handover of power.

events.title: Japarov and the return to presidential rule
events.summary: After 2020 protests over disputed elections, Sadyr Japarov rose from prison to the presidency, winning election in January 2021; constitutional referendums then restored a strong presidential system.

figures.name: Roza Otunbayeva
figures.blurb: A former diplomat who led the interim government formed after the 2010 ouster of Kurmanbek Bakiyev, overseeing the transition to a parliamentary republic. She stepped down after the 2011 election, in Kyrgyzstan's first peaceful transfer of power.

figures.name: Sadyr Japarov
figures.blurb: A nationalist politician freed from prison during the 2020 protests, Japarov rose rapidly to become president in January 2021 and oversaw constitutional changes restoring a strong presidential system.

pullquote.text: There are varying estimates of the death toll, with official figures of around four hundred and other estimates running considerably higher; all such figures remain estimates.
pullquote.attribution: On the June 2010 violence in southern Kyrgyzstan

figures.name: Manas
figures.blurb: The hero of the Kyrgyz epic Manas, celebrated as the warrior who united the forty Kyrgyz tribes; the epic that bears his name is the foundational text of Kyrgyz national identity.

figures.name: Chingiz Aitmatov
figures.blurb: Kyrgyzstan's most renowned author, writing in Kyrgyz and Russian. Works such as Jamila and The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years won the Lenin Prize and international fame, and he later served as a diplomat for the USSR and independent Kyrgyzstan.

figures.name: Askar Akayev
figures.blurb: Physicist who led Kyrgyzstan to independence in 1991 and served as its first president, presiding over an early era of relative openness before his overthrow in the 2005 Tulip Revolution.

figures.name: Roza Otunbayeva
figures.blurb: Led the interim government after the 2010 uprising and oversaw the move to a parliamentary republic, stepping down after the 2011 election in the country's first peaceful transfer of power.
